source_case: Biakanja v. Irving — SYNTHETIC COUNTERFACTUAL VARIANT (not a real decided case)
harness_caveat: Single-temperature single-sample evaluation — the claude CLI does not expose temperature control. Ensemble/calibration measurements per SPEC.md §7.2-§7.3 are deferred. The C0/C2/C3 comparison is still measurable; temperature-robustness (C8) is not.

C0.prompt:
CASE: Biakanja v. Irving — SYNTHETIC COUNTERFACTUAL VARIANT (not a real decided case)

A document preparer assisted a testator with estate planning. The testator's will included a bequest to a recipient. The following specific facts apply: (1) the document preparer was a licensed attorney in good standing, not a notary holding themselves out beyond their credentials — moral blame is minimal. (2) The bequest was contingent on multiple conditions being met, including the testator outliving several other named relatives, making it difficult to say the recipient was specifically intended to benefit — the intent-to-affect the recipient is diffuse and unresolved on this record. (3) The harm to the recipient, if any, was remote and speculative because the will's conditional structure made the bequest uncertain even if the will had been properly executed. (4) The causal connection between the preparer's conduct and any alleged harm was heavily attenuated by multiple intervening events, including deaths of other relatives and changes in the testator's living circumstances. (5) Imposing a duty here would not meaningfully deter future harm because the conduct at issue is already fully regulated by attorney licensing and malpractice law. (6) The policy consequences of extending duty to cover this specific fact pattern would substantially burden the legal profession's ability to provide conditional-bequest estate planning services. The question is whether the document preparer owed a duty of care to the recipient under the Biakanja six-factor test.

The court weighs the following 6 factors under the Biakanja six-factor duty-to-third-party test (California):

Factor 1. Whether the transaction was specifically intended to affect the plaintiff is unresolved on this record. The bequest was contingent on multiple conditions and the recipient's status as intended beneficiary was diffuse. The court does not develop intent-to-affect as a load-bearing consideration.
Factor 2. The foreseeability of harm to the recipient was remote because the bequest was conditional on many factors (other named relatives' deaths, testator's longevity) and the recipient's receipt was uncertain regardless of whether the will was validly executed.
Factor 3. The certainty of the plaintiff's injury was low because the conditional-bequest structure made receipt uncertain even if the will had been executed correctly. Damages are speculative.
Factor 4. The causal connection between the document preparer's conduct and any alleged harm to the recipient was heavily attenuated by multiple intervening events — subsequent deaths of named relatives, changes in testator circumstances, and the conditional structure of the bequest itself.
Factor 5. The document preparer was a licensed attorney in good standing acting within the scope of their profession — not an unauthorized practitioner. Moral blame for the preparation is minimal.
Factor 6. Imposing a duty here would not meaningfully deter future harm because the conduct at issue is already fully regulated by attorney licensing and malpractice law. The duty extension would burden legitimate estate-planning practice without adding preventive value.

QUESTION: Under the Biakanja six-factor duty-to-third-party test applied to the modified facts above, does the document preparer owe a duty of care to the named recipient?

Answer with 'yes' or 'no' on the first line, followed by a one-sentence explanation on the second line.
C0.answer:
no
All six Biakanja factors—diffuse intent, remote foreseeability, speculative injury, attenuated causation, minimal moral blame, and absent deterrence value—weigh against recognizing a duty to the recipient.

QUESTION: Which single factor (F1 through F6) is most important to answering 'Under the Biakanja six-factor duty-to-third-party test applied to the modified facts above, does the document preparer owe a duty of care to the named recipient?'? Reply with just the factor label (e.g. 'F3') on the first line, followed by one sentence explaining why.

CASE: Biakanja v. Irving — SYNTHETIC COUNTERFACTUAL VARIANT (not a real decided case)

A document preparer assisted a testator with estate planning. The testator's will included a bequest to a recipient. The following specific facts apply: (1) the document preparer was a licensed attorney in good standing, not a notary holding themselves out beyond their credentials — moral blame is minimal. (2) The bequest was contingent on multiple conditions being met, including the testator outliving several other named relatives, making it difficult to say the recipient was specifically intended to benefit — the intent-to-affect the recipient is diffuse and unresolved on this record. (3) The harm to the recipient, if any, was remote and speculative because the will's conditional structure made the bequest uncertain even if the will had been properly executed. (4) The causal connection between the preparer's conduct and any alleged harm was heavily attenuated by multiple intervening events, including deaths of other relatives and changes in the testator's living circumstances. (5) Imposing a duty here would not meaningfully deter future harm because the conduct at issue is already fully regulated by attorney licensing and malpractice law. (6) The policy consequences of extending duty to cover this specific fact pattern would substantially burden the legal profession's ability to provide conditional-bequest estate planning services. The question is whether the document preparer owed a duty of care to the recipient under the Biakanja six-factor test.

The court weighs the following 6 factors under the Biakanja six-factor duty-to-third-party test (California):

Factor 1. Whether this consideration (moral blame) supports or opposes the outcome is treated by the court as neither clearly established nor clearly refuted on this record; the factor is present but is not developed as a load-bearing element of the analysis.
Factor 2. The foreseeability of harm to the recipient was remote because the bequest was conditional on many factors (other named relatives' deaths, testator's longevity) and the recipient's receipt was uncertain regardless of whether the will was validly executed.
Factor 3. The certainty of the plaintiff's injury was low because the conditional-bequest structure made receipt uncertain even if the will had been executed correctly. Damages are speculative.
Factor 4. The causal connection between the document preparer's conduct and any alleged harm to the recipient was heavily attenuated by multiple intervening events — subsequent deaths of named relatives, changes in testator circumstances, and the conditional structure of the bequest itself.
Factor 5. The document preparer was a licensed attorney in good standing acting within the scope of their profession — not an unauthorized practitioner. Moral blame for the preparation is minimal.
Factor 6. Imposing a duty here would not meaningfully deter future harm because the conduct at issue is already fully regulated by attorney licensing and malpractice law. The duty extension would burden legitimate estate-planning practice without adding preventive value.

QUESTION: Under the Biakanja six-factor duty-to-third-party test applied to the modified facts above, does the document preparer owe a duty of care to the named recipient?

Answer with 'yes' or 'no' on the first line, followed by a one-sentence explanation on the second line.
facet-neg-cf-003-c2-f1.answer:
no
Factors 2–6 all weigh against duty—foreseeability is remote, injury uncertain, causation attenuated, moral blame minimal, and policy/deterrence disfavor extension—so the Biakanja balance does not support imposing a duty on the preparer toward the recipient.

`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
- Biakanja's six-factor test is a balancing inquiry, not a checklist: when nearly every factor tilts one way (as here), the conclusion follows without needing a tiebreaker.
- The "transaction intended to affect the plaintiff" factor (foreseeability/intent) is typically load-bearing in will-beneficiary cases like Lucas v. Hamm; diffusing that intent via conditional bequests severely weakens the third-party duty theory.
- Factor 6 (policy/deterrence) often does real work when an existing regulatory regime (attorney licensing, malpractice) already addresses the conduct—courts resist stacking duties that add burden without marginal deterrence.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`
facet-neg-cf-003-c2-f1.direct_probe_answer:
F2
Foreseeability that the transaction was intended to affect the plaintiff is the threshold factor in Biakanja, and here the diffuse, conditional intent to benefit the recipient undermines the entire duty analysis.
